summary:
The Eurasian steppe, stretching from the Pontic grasslands north of the Black Sea to the Kazakh steppe and beyond into Central Asia, was home during the second millennium BC to mobile pastoralist communities whose migrations shaped the genetic, linguistic, and cultural landscape of much of Eurasia. The [[event:1860|Sintashta culture]] and the related [[event:1861|Andronovo culture]] represented major steppe-based societies that combined semi-sedentary settlement with extensive herding of horses, cattle, and sheep, and whose warriors wielded bronze weapons and drove the earliest spoked-wheel chariots.

Movements of steppe peoples during this period contributed to language spread on a massive scale. The Indo-Iranian branch of the Indo-European language family expanded eastward and southward from the steppe, with ancestors of the Iranian-speaking peoples settling across Central Asia and with proto-Indo-Aryan speakers moving into the Indian subcontinent and contributing to the [[event:59|Vedic Period]] and the [[event:1849|Indo-Aryan migration]] into South Asia. Simultaneously, steppe contacts with Europe contributed genetic and cultural elements traceable in Bronze Age European populations.

The steppe nomads of this era were not simply raiders but participants in complex networks of exchange, supplying horses, furs, and metals in return for prestige goods from settled civilizations. Their mobility and military effectiveness, based on horse-riding, archery, and chariot warfare, made them formidable neighbors for all the major Bronze Age states. By the end of the second millennium BC, the transition to iron tools and weapons, combined with perfection of horse-riding cavalry techniques, would enable later steppe peoples such as the Scythians and Cimmerians to exert even greater influence across Eurasia.
